TextBlock.Text: A WPF-style splitter lab for column and row resizing, overlay splitter placement, alignment-driven target resolution, explicit resize behaviors, auto direction, keyboard nudging, and bounded pane sizing.
The main stage uses real GridSplitter instances instead of a generic sample host, so drag and keyboard resizing, alignment rules, and pane limits stay visible in one surface.
Switch presets, turn on grid lines, widen the editor canvas, or lower the increments for precise keyboard and pointer nudging without changing the splitter tree.
Left pane keeps a readable minimum while the dedicated splitter lane resizes against the center workspace.
This center lane behaves like a WPF editor canvas: it absorbs most width, but still yields to splitter drags and keyboard nudges.
Try dragging either rail, then click it and use arrow keys. Pane minimums prevent the shell from collapsing through the splitter.
Toolbar, tabs, or canvases usually live in the expandable center track.
Actual column widths stay visible in the side rail so this pane can act as a quick reference while the shell shifts.
Right pane mirrors a property inspector or supporting rail that should stay narrow enough to read but never disappear.
A horizontal splitter is usually easier to read as a timeline or detail separator. The top row keeps its own minimum height.
Arrow Up and Down resize this splitter after focus, which makes it useful for log panes, properties, or stacked design surfaces.
This row splitter uses explicit Rows mode so the preview behaves like a classic WPF horizontal chrome handoff.
These examples behave like WPF overlay splitters placed inside a content column. Alignment selects which adjacent definitions move.
The same overlay pattern can force a specific pair even if alignment would suggest something else.
Width is much smaller than height, so Auto behaves like a vertical column splitter.
Width is greater than height, so Auto behaves like a horizontal row splitter.
Auto keeps the view declarative: geometry decides whether the splitter behaves like a column or row splitter without changing the control tree.
This workbench intentionally mixes dedicated splitter lanes with overlay placement because both patterns are common in WPF Grid layouts.
• Dedicated-lane column splitters for classic three-pane shells
• Dedicated-lane row splitter for timeline, console, or utility layouts
• Overlay splitters that rely on HorizontalAlignment plus BasedOnAlignment
• Explicit PreviousAndCurrent, PreviousAndNext, and CurrentAndNext behavior samples
• Auto direction samples that switch between Columns and Rows from geometry alone
Drag uses the configured DragIncrement, so the stage can feel coarse for shell resizing or precise for design tools.
Keyboard arrows respect KeyboardIncrement after the splitter has focus. Click a rail first, then use Left and Right for columns or Up and Down for rows.
Hover and drag state are rendered directly by the control, so the rails expose their own interaction state without an external template.

This view is intentionally authored like a WPF control gallery: overlay splitters sit inside content columns, dedicated splitter lanes get their own narrow definitions, and the side rail reports actual layout output rather than static documentation.
